subject: Physics
options: Both the coils carry currents in the same direction.
Coils will attract each other, even if the supply is an ac source.
solution:
As the force between the two coils is attractive hence currents in the coil must be in the same direction and the force, $\mathrm{F} \propto \frac{\mathrm{I}_1 \mathrm{I}_2}{2 \pi \mathrm{d}}$
where $I_1$ and $I_2$ are the currents flowing through the coils.

B=μ0Ir2πR2,   r≤Rμ0I2πr,   r>R

subject: Physics
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{r}}=\mathrm{qvB}$
$\mathrm{mv}=\mathrm{qBr}$
$\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2$
$\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{\mathrm{q}^2 \mathrm{~B}^2 r^2}{\mathrm{~m}^2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{q}^2 \mathrm{~B}^2 \mathrm{r}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}}$
$\mathrm{E}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{q}^2 \mathrm{~B}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}}\right) \mathrm{r}^2$
$r^2 \propto E$

subject: Physics
solution:
In uniform magnetic field,
$\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{m} \nu}{\mathrm{qB}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}(\mathrm{~K} . \mathrm{E})}}{\mathrm{qB}}$
Since same K.E
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{R} \propto \frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{q}} \\& \therefore \frac{\mathrm{R}_{\text {deutron }}}{\mathrm{R}_{\text {proton }}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{d}}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}}}} \times \frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{d}}} \\& =\sqrt{2} \times 1 \\& \therefore \gamma_{\mathrm{d}}: \gamma_{\mathrm{p}}=\sqrt{2}: 1\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
As charge on both proton and deuteron is same i.e. ' $e$ '
Energy acquired by both, $E=\mathrm{eV}$ For Deuteron.
Kinetic energy, $\frac{1}{2} m V^2=e V$
[ $V$ is the potential difference]
$$
v=\sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m_d}}
$$
But $m_d=2 m$
Therefore, $v=\sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{2 m}}=\sqrt{\frac{e V}{m}}$
Radius of path, $R=\frac{m v}{e B}$
Substituting value of ' $v$ ' we get
$$
\begin{array}{r}
R=\frac{2 m \sqrt{\frac{e v}{m}}}{e B} \\
\frac{R}{2}=\frac{m \sqrt{\frac{e v}{m}}}{e B}
\end{array}
$$
For proton :
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{2} m V^2=e V \\
& V=\sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m}} \\
& \text { Radius of path, } R^{\prime}=\frac{m V}{e B}=\frac{m \sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m}}}{e B} \\
& R^{\prime}=\sqrt{2} \times \frac{R}{2} \quad \text { [From eq. (i)] } \\
& R^{\prime}=\frac{R}{\sqrt{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
solution:
Two identical circular loops P and Q, each of radius $r$, are separated by distance $r$ along their common axis. Current in P is $I$ and in Q is $4I$ (both clockwise as seen from O).
Point O is at the midpoint. The magnetic fields from each loop at O:
$B_1 = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + R^2)^{3/2}}$ (from P, pointing toward Q)
$B_2 = \frac{4\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + R^2)^{3/2}}$ (from Q, pointing toward P)
$B_{net} = B_2 - B_1 = \frac{3\mu_0 I R^2}{2(2R^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{3\mu_0 I}{4\sqrt{2}R}$
Direction: towards Q.

subject: Physics
solution:
For a magnetic dipole placed in a uniform magnetic field, the torque is given by τ→=M→×B→, and potential energy U is given as,

U=-M→·B→=-MBcosθ

When M→ is in the same direction as B→ then τ→=0 and U is minimum = -MB as θ=0o.⇒ Stable equilibrium is (b).

When M→ the opposite direction of B→, then τ→=0 and U is max = +MB
As, θ=180o ⇒Unstable equilibrium in (d)

subject: Physics
solution:
For loop $B=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{nI}}{2 \mathrm{a}}$ where, $a$ is the radius of loop.
Then, $B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 a}$
Now, for coil $\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \mathrm{nA}}{\mathrm{x}^3}$ at the centre $x=$ radius of loop
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{B}_2 &=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \times 3 \times(\mathrm{I} / 3) \times \pi(\mathrm{a} / 3)^2}{(\mathrm{a} / 3)^3} \\
&=\frac{\mu_0 \cdot 3 \mathrm{I}}{2 \mathrm{a}} \\
\therefore & \frac{\mathrm{B}_1}{\mathrm{~B}_2}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I} / 2 \mathrm{a}}{\mu_0 \cdot 3 \mathrm{I} / 2 \mathrm{a}} \\
\mathrm{B}_1: \mathrm{B}_2=1: 3
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
solution:
When the proton is rotated in a cyclotron its maximum velocity will be corresponding to the maximum radius of the circular path. We know that the radius of the circle in a magnetic field is given by,

r=mvBq⇒v=Bqrm.

The kinetic energy will be,

K=12mv2=B2q2r22m⇒K=12×1.6×10-192×0.622×1.6×10-27×1.6×10-13 MeV⇒K=18 MeV

options: Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is false
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is false but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are false
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are true
solution:
For a moving coil galvanometer, 

NiAB=kθ

The current sensitivity is given by 

Si=NABk

So, if N is doubled then, the current sensitivity is also doubled.

The voltage is given by 

V=BNARkθ

The voltage sensitivity is given by

Sv=NABkR

As, N is increased, R also increases. So, the voltage sensitivity does not change as NR remains constant.
